title: Burton and Speke — sources of the Nile

intro.title: Two Lakes and a Quarrel
intro.body: Richard Burton and John Hanning Speke crossed German East Africa in 1857-1859 searching for the source of the Nile. They reached Lake Tanganyika together; Speke alone pressed north and sighted Lake Victoria. The dispute over which lake was the source ended only with Speke's death in 1864.

stops.name: Zanzibar
stops.note: Departure preparations, December 1856 - June 1857. Burton hires Sidi Mubarak Bombay as headman.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Sultan's palace overlooking the harbour, two Englishmen in waistcoats arguing in a courtyard, porters loading crates.

stops.name: Bagamoyo
stops.note: Mainland departure 27 June 1857. The party of 132 porters strikes inland for the unknown lakes.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Equatorial beach at dawn, surf, long string of porters with chests on heads ascending into bush.

stops.name: Tabora
stops.note: Arab depot. Burton and Speke recover here from malaria. From Tabora, Speke strikes north alone on a rumour of a great water while Burton stays to convalesce.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Arab-style flat-roofed houses behind a thorn fence, one English officer setting off north with a small party while another rests under a verandah.

stops.name: Mwanza (Lake Victoria)
stops.note: 3 August 1858. Speke reaches the southern shore alone and looks out over the great lake he names for the Queen, claiming it as the Nile's source — a sighting Burton, back at Tabora, will dispute for the rest of his life.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Sunlit inland sea with low granite islands, a single European in a pith helmet on a boulder above the shore, porters resting in the foreground.
